name: Turieto Alto and Turieto Bajo Loop Trail
name_original: Pradera de Ordesa – circular desde San Antón por Turieto Bajo y Molinieto
description: Hike the moderate 7.4-mile Turieto Alto and Turieto Bajo Loop Trail through ancient forests and past waterfalls in Ordesa National Park.
region_intro: Hiking around Torla-Ordesa offers access to the dramatic landscapes of the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park in the Spanish Pyrenees. The region is characterized by towering limestone peaks, deep glacial valleys, and numerous waterfalls. Hikers will find a diverse terrain featuring lush forests, expansive meadows, and the pristine Ara River. Best hiking trails around Torla-Ordesa The most popular hiking route is Cola de Caballo via the Soaso Steps from the Ordesa Meadow, a difficult 11.1 miles (17.8 km) trail that takes about 5 hours 18 minutes to complete. This route leads to the iconic Cola de Caballo waterfall. Another top favourite among local hikers is Cola de Caballo by the Hunter's Trail – circular from the Ordesa Meadow, a difficult 12.2 miles (19.7 km) path. This challenging route offers panoramic views of the Ordesa Valley. Local hikers also love the Turieto Alto and Turieto Bajo Loop Trail, a 7.5 miles (12.0 km) trail leading through ancient forests and past waterfalls, often completed in about 4 hours 47 minutes. Hiking around Torla-Ordesa is defined by glacial valleys, limestone peaks, and cascading waterfalls. The extensive network offers options for easy family-friendly strolls as well as challenging mountaineering routes. The routes in Torla-Ordesa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 3000 reviews. More than 24000 hikers have used komoot to explore Torla-Ordesa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Torla-Ordesa?
A: Torla-Ordesa offers an extensive network of over 115 hiking trails, catering to a wide range of abilities. These routes explore the dramatic landscapes of the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, featuring towering peaks, deep glacial valleys, and lush forests.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly hiking options in Torla-Ordesa?
A: Yes, there are 16 easy routes in Torla-Ordesa, many of which are suitable for families. These trails often feature gentle paths through ancient forests and along the Ara River, providing accessible ways to enjoy the natural beauty of the region. For example, shorter, scenic paths are available directly from Torla-Ordesa village, such as those leading to the Ermita de Santa Ana or Puente de los Navarros.

Q: Which trails feature waterfalls in Torla-Ordesa?
A: The region is renowned for its numerous waterfalls. The most iconic is the Cola de Caballo (Horsetail Waterfall), which can be reached via the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e931317806/cola-de-caballo-via-the-soaso-steps-from-the-ordesa-meadow" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cola de Caballo via the Soaso Steps from the Ordesa Meadow&lt;/a&gt; route. Along this path, you'll also encounter the beautiful &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/322882" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soaso Steps Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt;. Another route, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938306948/turieto-alto-and-turieto-bajo-loop-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Turieto Alto and Turieto Bajo Loop Trail&lt;/a&gt;, also winds past several cascades.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in the area?
A: Yes, many trails in Torla-Ordesa are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e938306948/turieto-alto-and-turieto-bajo-loop-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Turieto Alto and Turieto Bajo Loop Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a moderate hike through forests and past waterfalls. For a more challenging circular experience with panoramic views, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e932279919/cola-de-caballo-by-the-hunter-s-trail-circular-from-the-ordesa-meadow" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cola de Caballo by the Hunter's Trail – circular from the Ordesa Meadow&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints to explore on a hike?
A: Torla-Ordesa is rich in dramatic natural landmarks. The Ordesa Valley itself, with cliffs soaring over 800 meters high, offers spectacular views. You can also explore impressive gorges like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3710757" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cirque de Gavarnie&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3395884" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circo de Pineta&lt;/a&gt;. High mountain passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/347445" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roland's Breach&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/347706" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tentes Pass&lt;/a&gt; provide breathtaking vistas of the Pyrenees.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Torla-Ordesa?
A: The best time for hiking in Torla-Ordesa is generally from late spring to early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is typically mild, and trails are clear of snow, making most routes accessible. Summer offers warmer temperatures, while spring and autumn provide vibrant foliage and fewer crowds.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: Absolutely. Torla-Ordesa offers 44 difficult routes for experienced hikers. These include high-altitude challenges like those leading to the Brèche de Roland, Le Casque du Marboré, and Taillon, which provide demanding ascents and unparalleled panoramic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e931565291/puerto-de-bujaruelo-col-des-tentes-y-col-des-especieres-circular-from-refugio-de-bujaruelo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Bujaruelo, Col des Tentes y Col des Espécières – circular from Refugio de Bujaruelo&lt;/a&gt; is another challenging option with significant elevation gain.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Torla-Ordesa?
A: The trails in Torla-Ordesa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 3000 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the dramatic and diverse natural features, including the towering limestone peaks, the pristine Ara River, and the spectacular waterfalls. The well-maintained paths and the variety of routes for all skill levels are also often highlighted.

Q: Is it possible to see wildlife while hiking in Torla-Ordesa?
A: Yes, Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park is home to diverse wildlife. Keep an eye out for chamois, marmots, and various bird species, including bearded vultures and golden eagles, particularly in the higher altitudes and more secluded valleys. The pristine natural environment provides excellent opportunities for wildlife spotting.

Q: Are there any hikes that explore deep canyons or valleys?
A: The region is famous for its deep glacial valleys and canyons. The Ordesa Valley itself is a prime example, and the Añisclo Canyon offers another impressive natural adventure with varying levels of difficulty. You can also explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/958526" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Otal Valley&lt;/a&gt;, which is known for its stunning natural beauty, for example, via the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e936965154/waterfall-and-circus-of-otal-from-bujaruelo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall and circus of Otal from Bujaruelo&lt;/a&gt; route.

Q: Do I need a permit to hike in Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park?
A: While most trails within Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park do not require a specific hiking permit for general access, certain areas or activities, especially during peak season or for overnight stays, might have restrictions or require prior authorization. It's always advisable to check the official National Park website or visitor centers for the most current regulations before your visit, particularly for popular routes or sensitive ecological zones.
tips: Located at the foot of the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, we find the beautiful town of Torla, in Huesca, Aragón, anchored on the heights. It still preserves its traditional Pyrenean architecture, and faces the Ara River.

The Pradera de Ordesa is a strategic point from which many routes start. There is a restaurant where the mountaineers go to pay homage at the end of the exercise. Right here you can buy bus tickets back to Torla.

Passing this waterfall you will find a monument to Lucien Breit, "the singer of Ordesa", a Frenchman who dedicated part of his life to photographing Ordesa at the end of the 19th century. The monument also indicates the proximity to the Pradera de Ordesa.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this loop trail?
faqs.answer: The Turieto Alto and Turieto Bajo Loop Trail is a moderate hike that generally takes around 4 hours and 47 minutes to complete the full 12 km (7.5 mi) loop. This duration can vary depending on your pace and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Turieto Alto and Turieto Bajo Loop Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail primarily traverses a mature, mixed Atlantic forest with towering pines, beeches, and firs, creating a lush, shaded environment. You'll encounter natural paths, some paved or cobbled sections, and moss-covered forest floors. The Arazas River runs alongside the Turieto Bajo section, offering picturesque views and waterfalls.

faqs.question: Is the Turieto Alto and Turieto Bajo Loop Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed on this trail, but they must be kept on a leash at all times. As this trail is located within the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, it's crucial to respect park regulations regarding pets to protect the local wildlife and environment.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is excellent for hiking during warmer months (spring, summer, early autumn) due to the extensive shade provided by the dense forest. This makes it a refreshing option when other, more exposed trails might be too hot. Autumn also offers beautiful fall foliage.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: While there isn't a specific permit required for this particular trail, it is located within the Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park. There are no general entrance fees for the park itself, but during peak seasons (typically summer and Easter week), private vehicle access to the Ordesa Meadow (Pradera de Ordesa) is restricted, and you'll need to use a shuttle bus from Torla. Always check current park regulations before your visit.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and what are the parking options?
faqs.answer: The trail typically starts from the town of Torla. Parking in Torla can be limited, especially during peak season. If you're planning to start from the Ordesa Meadow (Pradera de Ordesa), be aware of seasonal restrictions on private vehicle access, which may require you to park in Torla and take a shuttle bus.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several beautiful natural features and landmarks. Along the Turieto Bajo section, you'll follow the Arazas River and pass by picturesque waterfalls like the Fir Waterfall and the Tamborrotera Waterfall. You might also catch glimpses of impressive peaks such as Mondarruego and Tozal de Mallo. The Navarros Bridge is another notable point.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with any other well-known hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other trails. Notably, it covers a significant portion of the Senda Pirenaica - E25 (78%) and the Senda de Turieto Alto (56%), as well as the Senda de Turieto Bajo (49%). You'll also find smaller segments of the GR 11 and GR 15.2, among others.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I spot on the trail?
faqs.answer: The well-preserved ecosystem of the Ordesa Valley is home to diverse wildlife. While sightings are never guaranteed, if you're lucky and observant, you might spot animals such as sarrios (Pyrenean chamois) in the higher sections or various bird species within the dense forest.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Rated as moderate, the full loop might be challenging for very young children due to its distance and elevation gain. However, the

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Rated as moderate, the full loop might be challenging for very young children due to its distance and elevation gain. However, the Turieto Bajo section, which follows the Arazas River and offers beautiful waterfall views, is a more tranquil and shorter option (about 7 km, 2 hours from Torla to Pradera de Ordesa) that could be suitable for families with older children or those looking for a less strenuous walk.

faqs.question: What makes the Turieto Alto section different from Turieto Bajo?
faqs.answer: The Turieto Bajo section closely follows the Arazas River, offering more expansive views of the river and its waterfalls, like the Fir Waterfall. In contrast, Turieto Alto ascends through a denser, ancient forest, providing a more tranquil and immersive experience within the woods, though with fewer open vistas.
